name: Technical Deep-Dive (reference-PDF grounded: Attention Is All You Need)
description: 30-minute two-channel technical deep-dive on the Transformer architecture, grounded in test-fixtures/modes-corpus/papers/attention_is_all_you_need_1706.03762.pdf loaded as a Modes Manager reference file (reference_files_primary source contract, real hybrid retrieval). All expectedFacts below are directly verifiable in the paper's own text (checked against a live pdf-parse extraction during harness construction). 16 answer-button press points.

timeline.text: Thanks for joining — today we're doing a deep dive on the Transformer paper you were asked to read, Attention Is All You Need. Ready to get started?
Yes, ready — I read through it carefully.
Let's start simple — what architecture does the paper propose, and what does it deliberately avoid using?
__PRESS__

press.label: architecture-overview
press.canonicalQuestion: What architecture does the paper propose, and what does it deliberately avoid using?

timeline.text: It's based solely on attention, no recurrence or convolution needed.
How many identical layers are stacked in the encoder?
__PRESS__

press.label: encoder-layer-count
press.canonicalQuestion: How many identical layers are stacked in the encoder?

timeline.text: Six.
Quick tangent — did you have any trouble finding the reading room today?
No, it was easy to find, thanks.
Back to the paper — how many parallel attention heads does the model use, and what's the per-head dimension?
__PRESS__

press.label: multihead-count-dims
press.canonicalQuestion: How many parallel attention heads does the model use, and what's the per-head dimension?

timeline.text: Eight heads, 64-dimensional each.
What positional encoding does the paper use, and why sine and cosine functions?
__PRESS__

press.label: positional-encoding
press.canonicalQuestion: What positional encoding does the paper use, and why sine and cosine functions?

timeline.text: Sine and cosine functions of different frequencies, so relative positions can be represented as a linear function.
What BLEU score did the big Transformer model achieve on WMT 2014 English-to-German?
__PRESS__

press.label: bleu-en-de
press.canonicalQuestion: What BLEU score did the big Transformer model achieve on WMT 2014 English-to-German?

timeline.text: 28.4 BLEU, improving over the previous best by more than 2 BLEU.
And what about English-to-French?
__PRESS__

press.label: bleu-en-fr
press.canonicalQuestion: What BLEU score did the model achieve on WMT 2014 English-to-French?

timeline.text: 41.8, a new single-model state of the art.
What hardware and how long did training take for the base model?
__PRESS__

press.label: training-hardware
press.canonicalQuestion: What hardware and how long did training take for the base model?

timeline.text: Eight NVIDIA P100 GPUs, trained the base model for 100,000 steps, about 12 hours.
What optimizer did they use, and what were the beta values?
__PRESS__

press.label: optimizer-adam-betas
press.canonicalQuestion: What optimizer did they use, and what were the beta values?

timeline.text: Adam, with beta1 0.9 and beta2 0.98.
What dropout rate did they apply for the base model?
__PRESS__

press.label: dropout-rate
press.canonicalQuestion: What dropout rate did they apply for the base model?

timeline.text: Pdrop of 0.1.
Good. Let's do a short conceptual detour — outside the paper, what's your general understanding of why RNNs are hard to parallelize?
Because hidden state at time t depends on t-1, so you can't compute steps in parallel within one training example.
Right, and that's exactly the motivation section of the paper. What vocabulary size was used for the English-German byte-pair encoding?
__PRESS__

press.label: bpe-vocab-size
press.canonicalQuestion: What vocabulary size was used for the English-German byte-pair encoding?

timeline.text: About 37,000 tokens, shared source-target vocabulary.
Let's talk about label smoothing — what epsilon value did they use, and what's the tradeoff?
__PRESS__

press.label: label-smoothing
press.canonicalQuestion: What epsilon value did they use for label smoothing, and what's the tradeoff?

timeline.text: Epsilon of 0.1 — it hurts perplexity but improves accuracy and BLEU.
Earlier you told me the model uses 8 attention heads with 64-dimensional projections each — walk me through why that specific split keeps computational cost similar to single-head attention.
__PRESS__

press.label: long-range-multihead-cost-rationale
press.canonicalQuestion: Earlier you told me the model uses 8 attention heads with 64-dimensional projections each — walk me through why that specific split keeps computational cost similar to single-head attention.
press.notes: TRUE long-range recall: back-references the candidate's own answer at press B3 (~13 simulated minutes earlier) stating 8 heads / 64 dims, and asks the model to build on that earlier-stated fact rather than restate a different number. Also directly checked against the paper text: 'the total computational cost is similar to that of single-head attention with full dimensionality' because dk=dv=dmodel/h=64.

timeline.text: Because each head has a reduced dimension (64 instead of the full 512), the total compute across all 8 heads ends up similar to one full-dimensionality head.
Good. Let's talk about the decoder now — what's different about self-attention there compared to the encoder?
__PRESS__

press.label: decoder-masking
press.canonicalQuestion: What's different about self-attention in the decoder compared to the encoder?

timeline.text: The decoder masks out future positions so attention can't look leftward past the current position — preserves the auto-regressive property.
What's your own opinion — do you think a fully attention-based model like this generalizes well beyond translation?
__PRESS__

press.label: opinion-generalization
press.canonicalQuestion: Do you think a fully attention-based model like this generalizes well beyond translation?
press.notes: Opinion question anchored to a paper fact (constituency parsing experiment) — answer must not fabricate details beyond what the paper reports.

timeline.text: Yes — the paper itself shows it generalizing to English constituency parsing with both large and limited training data, so the architecture isn't translation-specific.
Let's cover regularization more broadly — besides dropout and label smoothing, does the paper mention anything else?
Those two are the main ones the paper calls out explicitly in the regularization section.
What's the total training time and step count for the big model variant?
__PRESS__

press.label: big-model-training-time
press.canonicalQuestion: What's the total training time and step count for the big model variant?

timeline.text: 300,000 steps, 3.5 days.
Last one for this section — how many warmup steps did they use for the learning rate schedule?
__PRESS__

press.label: warmup-steps
press.canonicalQuestion: How many warmup steps did they use for the learning rate schedule?

timeline.text: 4000 warmup steps.
Good, that mostly covers the paper's core numbers. Let's spend the remaining time on open discussion.
Sure, happy to keep going.
In your own words, what's the intuition behind calling this mechanism 'self-attention' rather than just 'attention'?
Because it relates different positions of a single sequence to each other, rather than attention between two separate sequences like encoder-decoder attention.
Good. One more open question — if you had to summarize the paper's main contribution in one sentence, what would you say?
__PRESS__

press.label: closing-summary
press.canonicalQuestion: If you had to summarize the paper's main contribution in one sentence, what would you say?
press.notes: Open-ended closing synthesis question near end of session (minute ~27-28) — greeting-failure canary, same class as Script A's A18.

timeline.text: It shows that a model relying entirely on attention, without recurrence or convolution, can outperform prior architectures on translation while training faster.
Great, that's a solid summary.
Thanks for your time today, that wraps the technical deep-dive.
Thanks, talk soon.
